coredata.dc:title: Pivalic acid based N-doped carbon dots for drug delivery and antioxidant behaviour
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Darwish, Hany W.
Mothana, Ramzi A.
Ganguly, S.
coredata.dc:description: Heteroatom doped surface engineered carbon dots has immense potential not only in their fluorescent sensing but also in nanocarrier for drug molecules and antioxidant behavior. Herein, we report a one-step thermal coupling synthesis of carbon dots from pivalic acid and naturally abundant gelatin (PGCDs). The as-synthesized PGCDs exhibited excellent colloidal stability, biocompatibility, and antioxidant activity. The surface of PGCDs was further modified with anticancer drug molecules to assess their drug delivery capabilities. The drug-loaded PGCDs demonstrated sustained release profiles, indicating their potential for controlled drug delivery applications. For understanding the drug delivery mechanism, the cumulative release data have been fitted into Weibull and Higuchi model. The PGCDs also showed pH sensitive release behavior where acidic environment has a better control in release profile compared to the alkaline pH. The concentration dependent radical activity shows more than 80% activity at below 1 mg/mL PGCDs concentration. Their low toxicity against live human cell lines could promote this PGCDs as a potential versatile nano-vector biomedical research area.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Colloidal stability
Antioxidant activity
Drug delivery

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of pivalic acid-gelatine carbon dots (PGCDs)
Assessment of antioxidant activity using DPPH Radical
Assessment of antioxidant activity using ABTS radical cation
In vitro cytocompatibility assessment of the PGCDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors extend their appreciation to Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2024R119), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia for funding this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic depiction of N-doped CDs synthesis and its purification.

(a) UV–vis spectrum of PGCDs in aqueous medium. Inset: PGCDs in daylight and under UV exposure (b) PL spectra of PGCDs at different excitations (c) Red shift of the emission peak with excitation wavelength (d) Long-term stability of PGCDs. Data were presented as mean ± standard deviation, n = 3 (e) Influence of UV exposure time on the fluorescence intensity of the PGCDs. Data were presented as mean ± standard deviation, n = 3 (f) XPS full survey spectra of PGCDs.
Size stability of the PGCDs in (a-b) PBS and (c-d) fetal bovine serum (FBS) showing consistence of the sizes up to 7 days.

Radical scavenging activities of PGCDs against (a) DPPH radicals, (b) ABTS radical cations (c) Different processes underlie PGCDs' ability to scavenge radicals. Data were presented as mean ± standard deviation, n = 3.
Schematic illustrations of drug loading, release, and drug-carbon dots interactions.

(a) UV–vis spectra of pure drug (DOX), PGCDs, and drug-PGCDs conjugate (b) Cumulative drug release plot at different pH (c) cumulative drug release behavior of PGCDs at different physiological conditions (d) Weibull model fitting at different pH release behavior (f) Higuchi model fitting at different pH release behavior.
Cell proliferation behaviour of PGCDs nanoparticles for 24 h and 72 h, respectively.
Previously reported different quantum dots derived from different precursors, their synthesis methods, doping, quantum yields and applicability.

head.ce:title: Pivalic acid based N-doped carbon dots for drug delivery and antioxidant behaviour

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, P.O. Box 2457, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, P.O. Box 2457, Riyadh, 11451, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacognosy, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, P.O. Box 2457, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmacognosy, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacognosy, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, P.O. Box 2457, Riyadh, 11451, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Waterloo, ON N2L 3G1, Canada.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, University of Waterloo

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Waterloo, ON N2L 3G1, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heteroatom doped surface engineered carbon dots has immense potential not only in their fluorescent sensing but also in nanocarrier for drug molecules and antioxidant behavior. Herein, we report a one-step thermal coupling synthesis of carbon dots from pivalic acid and naturally abundant gelatin (PGCDs). The as-synthesized PGCDs exhibited excellent colloidal stability, biocompatibility, and antioxidant activity. The surface of PGCDs was further modified with anticancer drug molecules to assess their drug delivery capabilities. The drug-loaded PGCDs demonstrated sustained release profiles, indicating their potential for controlled drug delivery applications. For understanding the drug delivery mechanism, the cumulative release data have been fitted into Weibull and Higuchi model. The PGCDs also showed pH sensitive release behavior where acidic environment has a better control in release profile compared to the alkaline pH. The concentration dependent radical activity shows more than 80% activity at below 1 mg/mL PGCDs concentration. Their low toxicity against live human cell lines could promote this PGCDs as a potential versatile nano-vector biomedical research area.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a kind of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles that possess dimensions smaller than 10 nm. These nanoparticles exhibit remarkable chemical stability, excellent biocompatibility, and a high degree of water dispersibility. Consequently, they have found extensive utilisation in various biomedical applications [1]. The initial identification of these entities occurred serendipitously during the purification procedure of single-walled carbon nanotubes. Presently, they can be readily acquired from various sources and through multiple convenient methodologies, including stepwise organic synthesis from smaller precursor molecules, synthesis facilitated by microwave irradiation, treatment under hydrothermal or solvothermal conditions, electrochemical exfoliation, and liquid phase exfoliation, among others [2,3]. There are three distinct subtypes of CDs that may be identified: graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots, and carbonised polymer dots [4]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and carbonised polymer dots (CPDs) generally exhibit spherical architectures and are commonly synthesised by means of assembly, crosslinking, polymerization, and carbonization processes involving small molecules, polymers, or biomass materials. CQDs have multi-layered graphite structures, whilst carbonised polymer dots possess distinctive core-shell architectures [5]. The carbon cores of carbonised polymer dots typically have a diameter of less than 10 nm. These cores are surrounded by shells composed of different functional groups or polymer chains. This structural arrangement imparts favourable stability and minimal toxicity to the carbonised polymer dots.

CDs have been effectively utilised in several fields such as bioimaging [6–8], catalysis [9,10], sensors [11–15], drug administration [16–19], and other applications [20–22]. In particular, there has been significant advancement in the use of CDs for drug encapsulation. The phenomenon of drug-induced toxicity to normal cells resulting from non-specific drug delivery is widely acknowledged [17]. However, a promising solution to mitigate this issue lies in the utilisation of CDs delivery mechanism. The Jia group employed polyethylene glycol (PEG) modified CDs to facilitate the targeted delivery of pharmaceuticals to the tumour microenvironment. This approach effectively minimised drug leakage in physiological conditions and mitigated the adverse effects associated with the treatment. Consequently, the anticancer efficacy of doxorubicin (DOX) was enhanced [23]. Despite the existence of numerous studies focused on the synthesis and surface functionalization of carbon dots, as previously indicated, there is a scarcity of literature regarding the drug delivery capabilities of carbon dots [24,25].

In contemporary times, significant emphasis has been placed on the advancement of an environmentally sustainable method for the synthesis of luminescent CDs by the utilization of naturally occurring precursors [26]. Inspiring from these, naturally obtained polymer, gelatin has been taken as the main precursor material for synthesizing CDs. Gelatin, a biodegradable protein obtained from the partial hydrolysis of collagen, has garnered significant attention across many industries including photography, pharmaceuticals, and cosmetics. These advantageous characteristics include a high degree of water solubility, a thermo-reversible sol-gel transition, non-toxicity, exceptional mechanical strength, and elasticity while in a dry condition [27]. Moreover, gelatine is highly prone to functionalize with other molecules for better tailor-made features. Thus, thermal coupling of gelatine and pivalic acid has been carried out to synthesize N-doped CDs. Herein, we demonstrated the facile and simple fabrication strategy of luminescent CDs derived from naturally obtained polymer, gelatin and pivalic acid, as well as their application for controlled drug delivery and antioxidant behaviour. To the best of our knowledge pivalic acid is used here for the first time as carbon source which was thermally coupled with gelatin for N-doped CDs synthesis. These CDs unveiled excitation dependent emission behaviour, excellent photostability, non-toxicity against human epithelial corneal cell line (HCEC). Due to the excellent features of PGCDs, including bright fluorescence, water solubility, nontoxicity, and good colloidal stability, they were investigated for potential use in drug delivery. The CDs were also studied in different physiologically simulated fluids and fitted into two different models (Higuchi and Weibull). Both models show more than 95% of goodness of fit. Moreover, the models could imply diffusion controlled drug carrier behaviour of the PGCDs. These PGCDs played a vital role in DOX loading and sustained release, which exhibited excellent performance for nanotherapeutic cargo compared to other works reported earlier [18,28]. Additionally, the PGCDs demonstrated high antioxidant activity with both 2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl and 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) assays (>80%). To our knowledge, this is the first attempt to demonstrate the possible use of PGCDs for antioxidant and drug delivery applications.

Pivalic acid, gelatine and quinine sulphate were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, Germany. The water for using the whole experiments was purified through a Millipore system (Bedford, MA, USA). Other chemicals used here were of analytical grades and were used without further purification. 2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), and 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. All other chemicals used here are of analytical grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of pivalic acid-gelatine carbon dots (PGCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The N-doped CDs were synthesised through the application of thermal coupling to a mixture of pivalic acid (PA) and gelatine. 36 mg of PA and 185 mg of gelatine were dissolved in 10 mL of water and then evaporate to dryness followed by charring in a borosilicate glass vial. Next, 15 mL of distilled water was poured into the vial and kept ∼10 min at 50 °C. After that, the glass vial was cooled to the room temperature and stirred for 20 min followed by centrifugation at a speed of 12000 rpm for duration of 15 min in order to isolate the larger particles. The suspension was dialyzed against Milli-Q water using a semi-permeable membrane (cellulose ester membrane bag with an average molecular weight of 500–1000 Da) for 48 h. During dialysis, the dispersion was subject to constant stirring and the water was replaced every 10 h. The PGCDs were subsequently subjected to freeze drying under vacuum conditions for 2 days, resulting in the formation of a brownish powdered substance with a yield of around 32%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of antioxidant activity using DPPH Radical
ce:section.ce:para: DPPH was used to assess the free radical scavenging capabilities of various concentrations of PGCDs. The details have been given in supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of antioxidant activity using ABTS radical cation
ce:section.ce:para: The ABTS radical cation decolorization test was used to determine the free radical scavenging activity[29]. The details have been given in supporting information.

Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX), a drug that dissolves easily in water, was combined with an aqueous solution containing 12 mg of DOX per 100 mL of solution (w/V). This DOX solution was then mixed with an aqueous dispersion of PGCDs, which contained 50 mg of PGCDs per 100 mL of solution (w/V). The mixed system was agitated in a 100 mL beaker for duration of at least 10–14 hours. During the process of stirring, the mixture of the medication and PGCDs was tightly sealed using a Parafilm stretch wrapper. Additionally, the beaker was covered with aluminium foil to provide protection against direct sunlight. The mixture was subjected to centrifugation at a speed of 10000 rpm for duration of 20 min to separate any desired insoluble solid particles. The entire liquid portion containing the drug-PGCDs was then gathered and placed into a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 10 kDa. It was then subjected to dialysis against Milli-Q water for duration of three consecutive days. The drug loading was quantified by collecting the supernatant and estimating it based on the calibration plot of DOX at 480 nm (λmax). The drug loading efficiency was determined by this equation:
(1)
Drug loading efficiency ( % ) =
Amt . of drug − PGCDs conjugate
− ( Amt . of free DOX )
Amt . of DOX
× 100

The release mediums used were phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), SGF (simulated gastric fluid, consisting of HCl, NaCl, and pepsin, with a pH of 1.3), and SIF (simulated intestinal fluid, containing NaOH, KH2PO4, and trypsin, with a pH of 7.5). ∼2 mg of drug-loaded nanoparticles were evenly distributed into a 20 mL solution of phosphate-buffered saline, while maintaining the previously specified pH. Next, 10 mL of the dispersion was carefully placed into a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 100–200 Da. The dialysis bag was then submerged in a 500 mL glass beaker filled with a PBS solution. The system was maintained at a consistent temperature of approximately 35–37 °C. While agitating the system, the entirety of the beaker was enveloped by an aluminium sheet. At regular intervals, a 1.5 mL sample was extracted and placed in a quartz vial. The sample was then analysed using a UV–vis spectrophotometer with a maximum wavelength of 480 nm. The diffusion of the DOX through the dialysis membrane was used as the control. The cumulative drug release (CDR) was determined by this equation:
CDR = [(Total absorbance – Initial absorbance)/Total absorbance] × 100(2).
Where, total absorbance and Initial absorbance stand for the absorbance intensity at different time interval (in min.) and absorbance at time = 0 min.

The synthesis of gelatine and pivalic acid-based CDs (PGCDs) was achieved by a single-step pyrolysis process. The thermal coupling of gelatine and pivalic acid was achieved through carbonisation. The presence of functional groups in the samples resulted in the formation of amide groups, as determined by the surface characterizations. The whole synthesis approach is depicted in Fig. 1.

The UV–visible spectra ( Fig. 2a) of PGCDs exhibited peak at 276 nm which attributed to the π–π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domain of PGCDs. Inset showed that under daylight, the aqueous dispersion of PGCDs exhibited a light yellow colour, while under UV light, it displayed a blue fluorescence. The emission spectra of PGCDs, ranging from 416 to 472 nm, revealed a change in the fluorescent emission signal as the excitation wavelength was altered from 320 to 420 nm (Fig. 2b). Fig. 2c displays the red-shifted emission of PGCDs under different excitation wavelength. The observed emission behaviour, which is dependent on excitation, aligns with earlier findings[30,31]. This phenomenon arises due to the presence of surface-emissive traps and the influence of the size effect on the band gap of CDs. CDs exhibit advantages over conventional organic dyes in terms of their minimal photobleaching, chemical stability, and ability to dissolve in water. Fig. 2d demonstrates that the fluorescence intensity of PGCDs remains above 98% of its initial intensity even after being stored at 4 °C for 20 days. To keep the PGCDs solution for extended periods, it can be dried in a vacuum oven to obtain a powder form. This powder can then be redispersed in water before being used. The photostability of PGCDs was examined by subjecting them to continuous UV light exposure for duration of 120 min. The fluorescence signals of PGCDs were recorded at various time intervals. No notable alteration (Fig. 2e) was seen in the emission intensity of PGCDs even after 180 minutes of uninterrupted UV irradiation. In addition, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted to analyse the elemental composition and surface states of PGCDs. The XPS spectra (Fig. 2f) exhibited three prominent peaks at 285.09, 400.44, and 532.91, which corresponded to the presence of C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively [32]. Polar functionalities in PGCDs enhance their stability in polar solvents, making them advantageous for applications as fluorescence probes. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted to analyze the morphology of PGCDs. The high resolution (HRTEM) image (Figure S1) clearly shows that PGCDs exhibited a quasispherical morphology and were well dispersed having an average size of about 3 nm. Zeta potential tests were performed to evaluate the stability of the colloidal fluorescent PGCDs in solutions. The average zeta potential of the PGCDs was found to be −23.5 mV at physiological pH=7.4. The result suggests that these nanoparticles have good stability as reflected by the high negative charges that prevent agglomeration due to inter-particulate electrostatic repulsion forces.

Table 1 shows a comparison of our work with that of various other researchers. Table 1 demonstrated that synthesis method, doping, quantum yield and various biomedical applications of CDs for different precursors. The table shows that our as-prepared PGCDs have superior quantum yields to some previously reported nitrogen doped CDs. Additionally, the PGCDs derived from naturally obtained gelatin and pivalic acid imparts excellent photostability and retain its property for long-term storage.

The assessment of nanoparticle size stability is a crucial factor in colloidal systems. In this study, we have conducted a more thorough assessment of the stability of PGCDs in an aqueous dispersion. The dimensions of the PGCDs have been determined based on the plot obtained from the dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiment, as depicted in Fig. 3(a–d). At first, the DLS data confirmed that the average diameter of the PGCDs was approximately 2.5 nm. The systematic measurement of size also holds another importance in terms of stability. In addition, the DLS study was also assessed for duration of 3 weeks, which led to minimal alterations in the diameter of the PGCDs. This suggests that the synthesised PGCDs have exceptional stability in an aqueous environment, which also indicates a lengthy "shelf-life" for the PGCDs. To enhance comprehension of size stability, we also conducted a similar experiment using fetal bovine serum (FBS) as the dispersing agent for the PGCDs. The implementation was necessary due to the fact that cell culture and other experiments conducted in vitro or in vivo often utilise FBS medium. Observations in FBS medium revealed that the mean size of the PGCDs was nearly the same as in water/PBS media. However, when PGCDs were stored for a long period in FBS media, the increase in hydrodynamic diameter was minimal compared to that in PBS or water. It can be postulated that the dominant adsorption of long chains of FBS on the surface cannot be excluded, considering its supramolecular nature. The DLS data showed a minimal increase in the size of PGCDs, which had a negligible impact on their quality.

This study investigated the antioxidant properties of PGCDs using DPPH and ABTS radicals. The DPPH free radical (FR) is commonly employed to analyze the antioxidant capability of nanoparticles. The DPPH free radical scavenging experiment was thus used to evaluate the antioxidant properties of PGCDs. A DPPH radical is frequently used to assess antioxidant activity based on spectrophotometric changes caused by the reaction of DPPH• and an antioxidant. This procedure is swift, simple, and economical. Because it includes nitrogen atoms with lone-pair electrons and is surrounded by three benzene rings, DPPH is a stable free radical. When DPPH reacts with an antioxidant, it accepts hydrogen radical to generate a DPPH-H complex and transforms from deep violet to pale yellow[47]. The absorbance at 517 nm against a blank could be used to assess the decrease of the DPPH radical. The dose-dependent DPPH radical-scavenging capacity of PGCDs was tested at concentrations ranging from 0 to 750 µg/mL; as depicted in Fig. 4a. The absorbance of DPPH decreased as the concentration of PGCDs gradually rose until it reached 550 µg/mL. Further addition of PGCDs cause only a minor change in antioxidant activity, eventually reaching saturation at ∼750 µg/mL. The color change from blue-green to colorless revealed that PGCDs have concentration-dependent ABTS•+ scavenging capabilities (Fig. 4b). It was also validated by a decrease in absorbance after gradually increasing the PGCDs concentration from 0 to 750 µg/mL. The scavenging results of ABTS•+ exhibited a dose-dependent relationship, with saturation occurring at a concentration of 550 μg/mL of PGCDs. The main factors contributing to the higher antioxidant activity in the ABTS approach are the hydrophilicity and effective dispersion of PGCDs in an aqueous media. The hydrogen transfer from the hydroxyl groups of PGCDs can effectively decrease and quench the free radicals ABTS• and DPPH•. In order to enhance comprehension of the interaction, the radical scavenging mechanism employed by CPCDs was illustrated. Surface nitrogen and oxygen functionalities are thought to be implicated in the scavenging of reactive oxygen species (ROS)[48,49]. This phenomenon may be ascribed to the covalent radius and valence states. A correlation has been established between the sp2 carbon domains and electron-donating doped sites and the primary radical scavenging sites. These sites are critical in free radical annihilation via adduct formation and spin delocalization across the conjugated carbon backbone [48,50]. Dopants can enhance the radical scavenging reaction by donating electrons to radicals, which is then followed by either proton transfer or the creation of an adduct [31,51]. Indeed, the ROS scavenging activity of PGCDs may be linked to the H-donor behavior of edge-site functional groups such as OH, COOH, and NH2 in conjunction with free electron delocalization and stabilization at the sp2-hybridized carbon network. Fig. 4c depicts the comprehensive idea of the radical scavenging action of PGCDs [50].

At present, chemotherapy is the primary method of treating cancer, and DOX is among the often prescribed chemotherapy medications. The efficacy of chemotherapy in advanced cancer was constrained by its significant toxicities. A significant amount of resources has been dedicated to researching the delivery method and enhancing the precise release of DOX in order to minimise its adverse effects. According to the paper, the pH level in the tumour tissue ranges from 6.5 to 7.2, indicating a mild acidic environment. However, the lysosomes within the tumour cells are even more acidic. This acidic condition serves as an optimal stimulus for the regulated release of drugs [52]. Several researchers have created numerous pH-responsive drug-loading devices to provide controlled release of drugs [53]. It is widely recognised that the small intestine maintains a neutral pH and serves as the primary site for the absorption of nutrients. Typically, food remains in the stomach for a duration of 2–3 h before it is assimilated into the small intestine [54]. We conducted an experiment to investigate the release patterns of PGCDs-DOX in different pH conditions, specifically in PBS buffer, SGF, and SIF. The release of PGCDs-DOX ( Fig. 5) exhibited pH-dependency in comparison to DOX. At 72 h, the release of PGCDs-DOX was 65%, 21%, and 12% at pH values of 5.2, 6.6, and 7.4 respectively. This might be attributed to the instability of the –CN– bond in CDs-DOX, which makes it prone to dissociation in an acidic environment. CDs-DOX shown a greater release capacity in simulated gastric fluid (SGF) with a pH of 1.3 compared to plasma with a pH of 7.4 and simulated intestinal fluid (SIF) with a pH of 7.5. This can be attributed to the notably acidic pH of SGF.

The release of CDs-DOX on the tumour surface was replicated in a PBS medium (pH = 5.0) in vitro. The release of drugs has been confirmed from their UV spectra as shown in Fig. 6a. The release data was fitted using two mathematical models, namely the Weibull model and the Higuchi model. The Weibull model is a mathematical model that describes the accumulation of drug release as time progresses. The model is represented by the following equation:
(3)
F
t
= 1 − exp ⁡
−
t
λ
β
Where F(t), λ, and β correspond to the cumulative drug release at time t, scale parameter, and shape parameter; respectively. When utilising the Weibull model for drug release kinetics, it is imperative to take into account the drug delivery system's type, the formulation features, and the experimental conditions. Scientists commonly conduct experiments to quantify the release of drugs at various time intervals, and subsequently employ regression analysis to fit the data to the equation of the Weibull model.

The Higuchi model is a frequently employed mathematical model for describing the release of drugs from pharmaceutical dosage forms, especially when diffusion is the main factor controlling drug release. This concept is founded upon Fick's law of diffusion and postulates that drug release transpires via the process of drug dissolution.

The Higuchi model is articulated by the following equation:
(4)
Q
t
= k .
t
1
2

Here Q(t) and k are the cumulative drug release at time t and Higuchi constant representing drug release rate, respectively. In the Higuchi model, the square root of time represents drug release that is directly proportional to the square root of time. This property is indicative of diffusion-controlled release mechanisms. It is commonly employed that the practice of graphing the cumulative drug release (Q(t)) versus the square root of time when fitting experimental data to the Higuchi model. If the release of the drug adheres to a diffusion-controlled mechanism, the resulting graph should exhibit a linear correlation. The cumulative release profile of the drug molecules is depicted in Fig. 6b,c at different pH and different physiological simulated solutions, respectively. The gradient of the linear segment of the graph is subsequently utilised to compute the Higuchi constant, which imparts insights on the rate at which the drug is released. Based on the fitting data presented in Fig. 6d, it is evident that the fitting coefficient (R2) for the Weibull model was the highest (R2 = 0.994) compared to the Higuchi model. This indicates that the drug release of PGCDs-DOX may be attributed to the Weibull model. Moreover, the shape parameter in Weibull model also has significant impact over the release mechanism of drug molecules. Theoretically, there are three considerations in this content; for β<1, the release behavior is considered as initial burst release following typical 1st order kinetics. In case of β=1; the release is zero order and for the β>1, the release behavior is more like sustained sigmoidal typed. In this case, all the release fittings data show little higher than 1 which could imply its time dependent sustained release behavior. Specifically, the lower pH data shows better controlled over the higher pH environment. Conducting in vitro drug release studies can assist in predicting the efficacy of drugs when used in vivo. For the Higuchi model, the fitted data is shown in Fig. 6e.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytocompatibility assessment of the PGCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 depicts the results of an in vitro investigation on the growth of human epithelial corneal cells. The study examined the effects of PGCDs nanoparticles and a tissue culture plate (TCP) on cell growth after 24 h and 72 h. The cell viability was assessed by measuring the extent of cell death in the presence of the sample. The vitality of cells is directly correlated with their metabolic activity. The MTT assay revealed minimal cell death. The number of cell killings was directly proportional to the concentration of PGCDs, as seen in Fig. 7. The extent of cell death showed negligible variations. After 24 and 72 h, the cell morphology remained unchanged, indicating favourable cell growth and attachment in the presence of nanoparticles. This study suggests that fibroblast cell lines do not exhibit cytotoxic effects.

In this study, we reported a simple, rapid, an effective one-step method for the synthesis of N-doped PGCDs. The antioxidant/radical scavenging activity of PGCDs was also meticulously studied, using both the DPPH and ABTS analysis. The PGCDs showed superior antioxidant activity for both the methods (>80%). In addition, PGCDs served as an example of a nano-vector for the release study of the model medication DOX. The doped PGCDs demonstrated pH-responsive release behaviour, with the release behaviour shown to be more controlled at pH 7.4 than at pH 5.2 and 6.6. In vitro cytotoxicity assessment of the PGCDs revealed negligible cell death and the cell morphology remained unchanged after 24 and 72 h, demonstrating that nanoparticles can promote cell proliferation and attachment. The fabrication of a nontoxic fluorescent nanodot, capable of responding to stimuli to release drugs and serving as an antioxidant nanomaterial, can potentially lead to the development of an intelligent and versatile material.

Ramzi A. Mothana: Data curation, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Sayan Ganguly: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Hany W. Darwish: Writing – original draft, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We hereby inform that there are no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2024R119), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia for funding this work.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.133595.

Science
